name: Taj Mahal
description: The Taj Mahal is an ivory-white marble mausoleum built by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. Completed in 1643 CE, it is India's most iconic UNESCO World Heritage Site and a supreme example of Mughal architecture.

hotspots.label: Quranic Calligraphy
hotspots.content: The arched entrance is adorned with verses from the Quran inlaid in black marble, a masterpiece of Pietra dura craftsmanship.

hotspots.label: Hauz-i-Kausar Pool
hotspots.content: The central reflecting pool divides the gardens into four quadrants symbolising the four rivers of paradise in Islamic theology.

hotspots.label: Central Dome
hotspots.content: The main dome rises 73 metres and is surrounded by four chattris. The double-shell construction was an architectural innovation of the era.

hotspots.label: Pietra Dura Inlay
hotspots.content: Thousands of semi-precious stones — lapis lazuli, turquoise, jade, and carnelian — are inlaid into white marble in elaborate floral patterns.

name: Qutb Minar
description: The Qutb Minar is a 73-metre soaring minaret of red sandstone begun by Qutb-ud-din Aibak in 1193 CE. It forms the centrepiece of the Qutb complex, India's oldest surviving mosque and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

hotspots.label: Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque
hotspots.content: India's earliest mosque, built using materials from 27 Hindu and Jain temples demolished by Qutb-ud-din Aibak after the Delhi Sultanate's victory.

hotspots.label: Five-Storey Minaret
hotspots.content: The minaret's five storeys each have a different design — alternating angular and rounded fluting, topped by carved stalactite corbels.

hotspots.label: 7th-century Iron Pillar
hotspots.content: A 7-metre iron pillar from the Gupta period (c. 375–415 CE) stands rust-free for over 1600 years, a marvel of ancient metallurgy still not fully explained.

name: Red Fort
description: The Red Fort is a massive Mughal fortification of red sandstone built by Emperor Shah Jahan as his palace and seat of government in 1648 CE. Every year on Independence Day, India's Prime Minister hoists the national flag from its Lahori Gate.

hotspots.label: Lahori Gate
hotspots.content: The principal entrance faces Lahore (now Pakistan). Its balcony is the site of the Prime Minister's Independence Day address every 15th August.

hotspots.label: Marble Throne Dais
hotspots.content: Shah Jahan received public petitions here. The marble dais once held the legendary Peacock Throne, later looted by Nadir Shah in 1739.

hotspots.label: Arcade Columns
hotspots.content: Sixty columns of red sandstone support the arcade where citizens would gather. Each column is a study in geometric precision.

name: Hampi Ruins
description: Hampi is the ruined capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, once one of the richest cities in the world. Spread across boulder-strewn landscapes along the Tungabhadra River, its temples, elephant stables, and royal enclosures form a surreal UNESCO World Heritage Site.

hotspots.label: 50-metre Gopuram
hotspots.content: The main tower of Virupaksha Temple rises 50 metres and is one of the oldest functioning temples in Hampi, dedicated to Shiva. The temple has been in continuous worship for over 700 years.

hotspots.label: Stone Chariot of Garuda
hotspots.content: The iconic stone chariot with rotating stone wheels is a shrine to Garuda and is the symbol on India's 50-rupee note.

hotspots.label: Musical Pillars
hotspots.content: The 56 musical pillars of the Ranga Mandapa produce different musical notes when tapped — a feat of acoustic engineering from the 15th century.

name: Ajanta Caves
description: The Ajanta Caves are 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments carved into the basalt cliffs of a horseshoe-shaped gorge. Dating from the 2nd century BCE to 480 CE, they preserve some of the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian art and Buddhist iconography.

hotspots.label: Horseshoe Cliff
hotspots.content: The 30 caves arc along a 550-metre horseshoe bend in the Waghora River gorge. Most were rediscovered in 1819 by British officer John Smith during a tiger hunt.

hotspots.label: Padmapani Bodhisattva
hotspots.content: The Padmapani fresco — a Bodhisattva holding a lotus — is among the most celebrated paintings in the world. The eyes seem to follow the viewer across the chamber.

hotspots.label: Tempera Technique
hotspots.content: Ajanta painters used a tempera technique on dry plaster, mixing mineral pigments with animal glue. These paintings have survived 1500 years in a volcanic gorge.

name: Ellora Caves
description: The Ellora Caves are 100 rock-cut monuments spanning Buddhist, Hindu, and Jain faiths, carved between 600–1000 CE. The centrepiece is the Kailasa Temple — the world's largest monolithic rock sculpture — hewn from a single basalt cliff.

hotspots.label: Carved From One Rock
hotspots.content: Cave 16, the Kailasa Temple, required removing 200,000 tonnes of volcanic rock over 100 years. Workers carved downward from the cliff top — no scaffolding, no second chances.

hotspots.label: Ravana Lifting Kailash
hotspots.content: A colossal relief panel shows the demon-king Ravana shaking Mount Kailash — Shiva's abode — while the goddess Parvati clings to Shiva in alarm. The figures span nearly 6 metres.

name: Khajuraho Temples
description: The Khajuraho Group of Monuments are medieval Hindu and Jain temples renowned for their Nagara-style architecture and erotic sculptures. Built by the Chandela dynasty between 950–1050 CE, only 25 of the original 85 temples survive today.

hotspots.label: 30-metre Shikhara
hotspots.content: The Kandariya Mahadeva's tower (shikhara) soars 30.5 metres. Its 872 sculptures are arranged in three horizontal bands — the divine, the human, and the celestial.

hotspots.label: Erotic Sculpture Panels
hotspots.content: The mithuna (erotic couple) sculptures occupy only 10% of total carvings. Scholars interpret them as symbolic of the union of the soul with the divine, or as tantric meditation aids.

name: Konark Sun Temple
description: The Konark Sun Temple is a 13th-century masterpiece conceived as a colossal chariot of the sun god Surya, complete with 24 intricately carved stone wheels and seven stone horses. Built by King Narasimhadeva I of the Eastern Ganga dynasty around 1250 CE.

hotspots.label: Sundial Wheels
hotspots.content: Each of the 24 stone wheels (1.2 m diameter) functions as a sundial. The spokes cast shadows that tell the time with precision still marvelled by modern engineers.

hotspots.label: Deul Tower (Lost)
hotspots.content: The original deul (main sanctuary tower) is believed to have stood 70 metres tall but collapsed centuries ago. The sanctum contained the sun idol facing east to catch the first rays.

name: Shore Temple, Mahabalipuram
description: The Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram is one of the oldest structural stone temples in South India, built by Pallava king Narasimhavarman II around 700 CE. It stands at the edge of the Bay of Bengal and is part of a UNESCO site complex that also includes the famous Pancha Rathas.

hotspots.label: Bay of Bengal
hotspots.content: The temple's east face catches the sunrise over the Bay of Bengal. Ancient mariners used it as a lighthouse for navigation along the Coromandel Coast.

hotspots.label: Twin Shiva Shrines
hotspots.content: Two of the three shrines are dedicated to Shiva — one with the deity seated, one with the linga. The third enshrines Vishnu in his cosmic-sleep (anantasayana) pose.

name: Fatehpur Sikri
description: Fatehpur Sikri was the Mughal imperial capital built by Emperor Akbar between 1571–1585 CE and abandoned after just 14 years due to water scarcity. Its perfectly preserved red sandstone palaces, courts, and mosques make it a UNESCO World Heritage Site of rare completeness.

hotspots.label: Buland Darwaza
hotspots.content: At 54 metres, the Buland Darwaza (Gate of Magnificence) is the world's largest gateway. Built by Akbar to commemorate his conquest of Gujarat in 1572.

hotspots.label: Central Pillar Throne
hotspots.content: A single elaborately carved pillar in the centre supports a circular throne platform connected to four corner galleries by bridges. Akbar reportedly sat here to hold religious debates between scholars of all faiths.

name: Mehrangarh Fort
description: Mehrangarh Fort rises 122 metres above the blue city of Jodhpur on a perpendicular cliff, its massive walls studded with cannonball marks. Founded in 1459 CE by Rao Jodha, it houses a superbly curated museum of Rajput arms, paintings, and palanquins.

hotspots.label: Blue City of Jodhpur
hotspots.content: The indigo-painted houses below were traditionally homes of Brahmin priests. The colour was believed to repel insects and keep homes cool in the Thar Desert heat.

hotspots.label: Sheesh Mahal
hotspots.content: The Palace of Mirrors features thousands of tiny convex mirrors set in floral plasterwork. Candlelight once turned the ceiling into a glittering night sky for the maharaja's entertainment.

name: Amber Fort
description: Amber Fort is a majestic Rajput fort-palace complex overlooking Maota Lake near Jaipur. Built in 1592 CE by Raja Man Singh I, its blend of Hindu and Mughal architecture — the Diwan-i-Am, Sheesh Mahal, and Sukh Niwas — makes it a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

hotspots.label: Maota Lake Reflection
hotspots.content: The main courtyard is used as a parade ground. From here, elephants once carried royalty up the steep ramp. The lake below perfectly reflects the fort at dawn.

hotspots.label: 1 Million Mirror Fragments
hotspots.content: The ceiling and walls are encrusted with over a million convex glass fragments set in geometric and floral patterns. A single candle flame multiplied makes the chamber glow like a constellation.

name: Chittorgarh Fort
description: Chittorgarh Fort is the largest fort in India, spreading across 700 acres on a 180-metre high mesa. Three sieges — 1303, 1535, and 1568 CE — mark its history with legendary acts of Rajput sacrifice (jauhar), and it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

hotspots.label: 37-metre Victory Tower
hotspots.content: Built by Rana Kumbha after defeating the Malwa Sultanate in 1448 CE, the nine-storey tower is covered in 157 square metres of Hindu deities and battle narratives.

hotspots.label: Padmini's Chamber
hotspots.content: Tradition holds that Alauddin Khalji saw Rani Padmini's reflection in a mirror from here in 1303 CE — an act that precipitated his siege. The first jauhar is said to have been lit in the cellars below.

name: Golconda Fort
description: Golconda Fort was the diamond-trading capital of the Qutb Shahi dynasty, once the world's only source of diamonds including the Koh-i-Noor and Hope Diamond. Its sophisticated acoustic system — a clap at the base entrance echoes audibly at the summit 400 metres away.

hotspots.label: Acoustic Entry Clap
hotspots.content: A single hand-clap at this exact spot under the dome can be heard at the Bala Hissar pavilion at the summit, 400 metres away. The fort used this as a security system to warn guards of approaching visitors.

hotspots.label: Qutb Shahi Tombs
hotspots.content: The domed mausoleums of the seven Qutb Shahi kings are visible from here. Their black-domed tombs are set in a sprawling garden complex — the Golconda necropolis.

name: Mysore Palace
description: Mysore Palace is the official residence of the Wadiyar dynasty and the largest palace in India by area. Its Indo-Saracenic architecture blending Hindu, Muslim, Rajput, and Gothic styles and 96,000 bulbs that light its exterior during Dasara remain India's most visited non-heritage monument.

hotspots.label: 24-carat Gold Finial
hotspots.content: The central dome is topped with a 145-kg gold finial. The palace was rebuilt in 1912 after the wooden structure burned down during the Dasara celebrations of 1897.

hotspots.label: Cast-Iron Pillars & Tiffany Ceiling
hotspots.content: The octagonal Kalyana Mantapa has a stained-glass ceiling supported by cast-iron pillars. The floor tiles were imported from Scotland and Glasgow, while the paintings narrate the Dasara procession.

name: City Palace, Udaipur
description: The City Palace of Udaipur is a magnificent lakeside palace complex begun by Maharana Udai Singh II in 1559 CE. Sprawling across a ridge above Lake Pichola, it is the largest palace complex in Rajasthan, with 11 interconnected palaces built by successive maharanas over 400 years.

hotspots.label: Lake Pichola & Jag Mandir
hotspots.content: The artificial Lake Pichola was created in 1362 CE. The Jag Mandir island palace in its centre sheltered the young Mughal prince Shah Jahan when he rebelled against his father Jahangir.

hotspots.label: Three Seasons Mosaic
hotspots.content: Three massive glass and tile mosaics (5 metres each) depict peacocks representing the three seasons of Rajasthan — Summer, Monsoon, and Winter. Each mosaic uses 5,000 pieces of coloured glass.

name: Jaisalmer Fort
description: Jaisalmer Fort is a living fort — one of the world's largest — rising from the sands of the Thar Desert. Built in 1156 CE by Rawal Jaisal, its golden sandstone walls glow at sunset and house temples, havelis, and a still-functioning community inside its ancient walls.

hotspots.label: Thar Desert Panorama
hotspots.content: The golden sands of the Thar extend 200,000 sq km — the world's most densely populated desert. The fort's 99 bastions blend into the sand at sunrise, giving Jaisalmer its nickname Sonar Qila (Golden Fort).

hotspots.label: Jain Temples Inside Fort
hotspots.content: Seven Jain temples built between 12th–15th centuries stand within the fort walls. Their interiors of carved yellow sandstone and silver icons rival the finest temples of Rajasthan.

name: Gwalior Fort
description: Gwalior Fort is a formidable hill fortress that has been called 'the Pearl among fortresses in India' by Mughal emperor Babur. Perched on a 100-metre sandstone escarpment, it contains palaces, temples, and water tanks spanning 8th–18th centuries.

hotspots.label: Tiled Duck Frieze
hotspots.content: The Man Singh Palace (1508 CE) exterior is decorated with blue, yellow, and green glazed tile friezes of elephants, ducks, crocodiles, and plantain trees — vivid against the sandstone.

hotspots.label: Giant Jain Tirthankara Carvings
hotspots.content: Colossal rock-cut carvings of Jain Tirthankaras line the approach road, some standing 17 metres tall. They were defaced by Babur but restored in the 20th century.

name: Golden Temple
description: The Harmandir Sahib, popularly known as the Golden Temple, is the holiest Gurdwara and spiritual centre of the Sikh faith. Its upper floors are covered in 750 kg of pure gold and it sits at the centre of the sacred Amrit Sarovar (Pool of Immortality), feeding over 100,000 people a day in its langar.

hotspots.label: 750 kg Gold Facade
hotspots.content: The upper floors and dome are plated in 750 kg of 24-carat gold donated by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in the early 19th century. The gold catches the light and reflects in the sarovar at all hours.

hotspots.label: Holy Sarovar
hotspots.content: Devotees take a ritual dip in the pool which is believed to have healing properties. Guru Arjan Dev completed the marble surround and installed the Adi Granth inside the sanctum in 1604 CE.

name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
description: The Meenakshi Amman Temple is a vast Dravidian temple complex dedicated to the goddess Meenakshi and her consort Sundareswarar (Shiva). Its 14 towering gopurams covered in thousands of painted stucco figures are among the most elaborate examples of South Indian temple architecture.

hotspots.label: 14-storey Gopuram
hotspots.content: The eastern gopuram rises 51 metres and is encrusted with 1,510 painted stucco statues of gods, demons, animals, and humans from Hindu mythology — a visual encyclopedia of the faith.

hotspots.label: Testing Literary Merit
hotspots.content: Legend says the Sangam poets (300 BCE – 300 CE) tested manuscripts by dropping them into this tank. Only meritorious works would float to the golden lotus — a Tamil literary standard.

name: Brihadeeswarar Temple
description: The Brihadeeswarar Temple, built by Chola king Raja Raja I in 1010 CE, is a magnificent example of Dravidian architecture and one of the largest temples in India. Its 66-metre vimana (tower) is constructed from a single granite block weighing 80 tonnes.

hotspots.label: Nandi Monolith
hotspots.content: A 25-tonne monolithic Nandi bull guards the entrance — the second largest Nandi in India — carved from a single boulder and facing the main linga in the sanctum.

hotspots.label: Shadowless Vimana
hotspots.content: The tower is precisely aligned so that at noon, its shadow falls entirely on itself rather than the surrounding ground — a deliberate architectural feat to symbolise the self-contained power of Shiva.

name: Kedarnath Temple
description: Kedarnath Temple is one of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines of Shiva and one of the Char Dham pilgrimage sites. Situated at 3,583 metres in the Himalayas, it is accessible only by a 16-km trek or helicopter. The stone temple is believed to have been established by the Pandavas.

hotspots.label: Kedarnath Peak 6,940m
hotspots.content: The Kedarnath Peak (6,940m) looms directly behind the temple. The stone structure survived the catastrophic 2013 floods due to a massive boulder that deflected the debris — believers call it divine protection.

hotspots.label: Chorabari Glacier
hotspots.content: The Chorabari (Gandhi Sarovar) glacier lake sits 3 km above the temple at 3,900m. Its sudden breach in June 2013 sent a wall of water and debris down the Mandakini River, killing thousands.

name: Somnath Temple
description: The Somnath Temple on the Arabian Sea coast is the first and most revered of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines dedicated to Lord Shiva. Looted and destroyed 17 times by invaders over the centuries, it was most recently rebuilt in 1951 CE and consecrated by India's first Deputy Prime Minister Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel.

hotspots.label: Baan Stambha
hotspots.content: An arrow pillar (Baan Stambha) on the seafront points south and reads: 'There is no land between this point and the South Pole.' It stands as a symbol of Somnath's position at the edge of the known world.

hotspots.label: Chalukya-style Shikhara
hotspots.content: The current temple follows the Chalukya style of architecture with a 155-foot shikhara. Patel insisted the rebuilding use traditional Sompura craftsmen from Gujarat — the same community that built the originals.

name: Bidar Fort
description: Bidar Fort is a medieval Bahmani Sultanate fortress dating to the 15th century. The city of Bidar was capital of the Bahmani and later Barid Shahi kingdoms and is famed for Bidriware — a unique craft of zinc and copper inlaid with silver, practised exclusively here.

hotspots.label: Triple Moat Fort
hotspots.content: Bidar Fort has a triple moat system carved into laterite rock, making it one of the most defensively sophisticated forts of the Deccan Sultanate era.

hotspots.label: Coloured Tilework
hotspots.content: The Rangin Mahal features exquisite coloured tiles and mother-of-pearl inlay on its walls — a fusion of Persian, Deccani, and Hindu decorative styles unique to the Bahmani court.

name: Aga Khan Palace
description: The Aga Khan Palace is a grand Italian-arched palace built in 1892 CE by Sultan Mohammed Shah Aga Khan III. It gained profound historical significance when Mahatma Gandhi, Kasturba Gandhi, and Mahadev Desai were interned here during the Quit India Movement of 1942. Kasturba Gandhi and Desai died here.

hotspots.label: Italian Arcades
hotspots.content: The five-arched Italian-style arcade was built by Sultan Aga Khan III to provide famine relief employment to local people. He donated the palace to India in 1969 as a memorial to Gandhi.

hotspots.label: Kasturba's Samadhi
hotspots.content: Kasturba Gandhi died here on 22 February 1944 during her internment. Her white marble samadhi in the palace garden is a place of quiet pilgrimage. Mahadev Desai's samadhi stands beside it.

name: Vaishno Devi Shrine
description: The Vaishno Devi Shrine is one of the most visited Hindu pilgrimage sites, receiving over 8 million devotees annually. Located in the Trikuta Mountains at 1,560 metres, the 14-km trek from Katra leads to a holy cave housing three naturally formed rock formations (pindis) representing the Divine Mother.

hotspots.label: Trikuta Mountain Range
hotspots.content: The Trikuta Hills, at whose heart the shrine sits, are a spur of the Shivalik range. The name means 'three-peaked,' corresponding to the three pindis of Maha Kali, Maha Lakshmi, and Maha Saraswati.

hotspots.label: Three Pindis
hotspots.content: Inside the 30-metre long cave, three natural rock formations (pindis) represent the goddess in her three forms. No idol was installed — the pindis are the living embodiment of the Divine Mother.

name: Lingaraj Temple
description: The Lingaraj Temple is the largest temple in Bhubaneswar and the most important Shaivite temple of Odisha, dedicated to Harihara — a fusion of Shiva and Vishnu. Its 55-metre deula tower, built around 1090 CE, is the tallest structure in the city of temples.

hotspots.label: 55-metre Deula
hotspots.content: The deula (tower) of Lingaraj stands 55 metres and has a curvilinear profile typical of Kalinga architecture. The tower is covered in sculptural narratives including erotic mithuna couples.

hotspots.label: 50 Subsidiary Shrines
hotspots.content: Over 50 smaller temples crowd the outer courtyard of Lingaraj, dedicated to various deities. The complex covers nearly 7 acres and is one of the oldest living temple complexes in India.

name: Tirumala Venkateswara Temple
description: The Tirumala Venkateswara Temple atop Tirumala Hill is the world's most visited pilgrimage site, receiving 50,000–100,000 devotees daily and annually collecting the highest donations of any religious institution on earth. The presiding deity Venkateswara (Vishnu) is believed to have manifested here in the Kali Yuga.

hotspots.label: Gold-Plated Gopuram
hotspots.content: The main gateway tower is plated with gold contributed by devotees. The temple generates over ₹650 crore annually from donations and is a significant contributor to Andhra Pradesh's economy.

hotspots.label: Hair Offering Tradition
hotspots.content: Over 650 barbers work round the clock in the Kalyanakatta tonsure hall. Devotees offer their hair as a symbol of surrendering ego. The temple processes over 10 million kg of hair annually, sold to wig manufacturers worldwide.

name: Jagannath Temple, Puri
description: The Jagannath Temple in Puri is one of the four sacred dhams of Hinduism. The presiding deity Lord Jagannath (a form of Krishna/Vishnu) is famous for the annual Rath Yatra chariot festival, in which the deity is placed in a massive wooden chariot and pulled through the streets by thousands.

hotspots.label: Aruna Stambha
hotspots.content: The 16-sided monolithic pillar outside the Lion Gate was brought from the Konark Sun Temple. It is called the Aruna Stambha after Aruna, the charioteer of the sun god.

hotspots.label: 65-metre Nilachakra
hotspots.content: The Nilachakra (Blue Wheel) at the apex of the 65-metre tower is an 8-spoked wheel in Ashtadhatu (8-metal alloy), weighing over a tonne. It always appears to be facing you from any position — a mystery still debated.

name: Kapaleeshwarar Temple
description: The Kapaleeshwarar Temple in Mylapore is Chennai's oldest and most important Shaivite temple. Its 37-metre whitewashed and brightly painted gopuram, tank, and the legend of Parvati worshipping Shiva in the form of a peacock (mayil) give Mylapore its name.

hotspots.label: Multicoloured Gopuram
hotspots.content: The 37-metre gopuram is covered in over 500 stucco figures from Hindu mythology, painted in vivid colours and renewed every 12 years during the Kumbabhishekam festival.

hotspots.label: Arupathumoovar Festival
hotspots.content: During the annual Arupathumoovar (63 saints) festival, processions of floats and idols circumambulate the tank for ten days. Thousands of Nayanmars' images are carried through the streets of Mylapore.

name: Jim Corbett National Park
description: Jim Corbett National Park is India's oldest national park, established in 1936 as Hailey National Park. Located in the Himalayan foothills, it is home to the highest density of Bengal tigers in the world and was the launch site of Project Tiger in 1973.

hotspots.label: Dhikala Chaur
hotspots.content: Dhikala is a large grassland clearing (chaur) in the Patlidun valley, offering some of India's finest wildlife sightings — elephant herds, tigers, leopards, and gharial crocodiles at the Ramganga river edge.

hotspots.label: Gharial & Mugger Crocodiles
hotspots.content: The Ramganga River is home to both critically endangered gharial crocodiles (with long, narrow snouts) and mugger crocodiles. The gharial's narrow jaw is adapted exclusively for catching fish.

name: Kaziranga National Park
description: Kaziranga National Park in the Brahmaputra floodplain of Assam is home to two-thirds of the world's great one-horned rhinoceroses. Its UNESCO World Heritage status and Project Tiger designation protect an extraordinary density of wildlife in a mosaic of tall elephant-grass, marshland, and forests.

hotspots.label: Tall Elephant Grass
hotspots.content: The tall elephant grass (up to 4 metres) is the defining habitat of Kaziranga. It provides cover for over 2,400 one-horned rhinos — making this park home to the world's largest single rhino population.

hotspots.label: Brahmaputra Horizon
hotspots.content: The Brahmaputra is one of the world's few male-gendered rivers. During monsoon floods, animals swim to the Karbi Anglong Hills — rhinos, elephants, and tigers crossing the NH-37 highway in full view of motorists.

name: Sundarbans Delta
description: The Sundarbans is the world's largest tidal halophytic mangrove forest, shared between India and Bangladesh. This UNESCO World Heritage Site and Biosphere Reserve is the only remaining habitat for the Royal Bengal Tiger that has adapted to a semi-aquatic life, swimming between tidal islands.

hotspots.label: Prop Root Labyrinth
hotspots.content: Sundari trees (Heritiera fomes) give the Sundarbans its name. Their complex prop root systems trap silt, build land, and provide breeding grounds for over 300 species of fish and hundreds of bird species.

hotspots.label: Swimming Bengal Tiger
hotspots.content: The Sundarbans tiger is the only tiger subspecies that regularly swims open-sea channels up to 5 km. Locals wear face masks on the back of their heads — tigers reportedly do not attack when they feel watched from behind.

name: Valley of Flowers
description: The Valley of Flowers National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the Western Himalayas, famous for its vast meadows of endemic alpine flowers blooming in the monsoon season. The valley was first documented by British mountaineer Frank Smythe in 1931 after accidentally discovering it.

hotspots.label: 500+ Flower Species
hotspots.content: Over 500 species of wildflowers bloom here from July–September, including the Himalayan blue poppy, cobra lily, and Brahma Kamal (Saussurea obvallata) — Uttarakhand's state flower, believed to bloom only at night.

hotspots.label: Nanda Devi Massif
hotspots.content: The valley is framed by the glaciers and peaks of Nanda Devi Biosphere Reserve. Nanda Devi (7,816m) is India's second highest peak and is revered as the guardian goddess of the Garhwali people.

name: Munnar Tea Gardens
description: Munnar is a high-altitude hill station in the Western Ghats of Kerala, famous for its vast rolling tea estates covering 80,000 hectares in mist-cloaked hills. The Tata Tea plantations here produce some of India's finest CTC and orthodox teas.

hotspots.label: CTC Tea Rows
hotspots.content: The manicured tea bushes planted in ruler-straight rows are pruned to waist height for easy picking. Each bush can be harvested every 7–10 days; skilled pluckers collect over 20 kg of green leaf daily.

hotspots.label: Neelakurinji in Bloom
hotspots.content: The Neelakurinji (Strobilanthes kunthiana) shrub blankets the Munnar hills in blue-purple flowers — but only once every 12 years. The last mass blooming was in 2018; the next is expected in 2030.

name: Coorg Coffee Estates
description: Coorg (Kodagu) in the Western Ghats is India's largest coffee-producing district, blanketed with Arabica and Robusta plantations under shade trees. The Kodava people, known for their martial traditions, unique cuisine, and distinctive language, have farmed these misty hills for centuries.

hotspots.label: Arabica Under Shade
hotspots.content: Coorg coffee is shade-grown beneath silver oak and pepper vines in an agroforestry system that produces premium Arabica beans. The intercropped cardamom and pepper fill the air with a spice-coffee fragrance.

hotspots.label: 70-metre Waterfall
hotspots.content: Abbey Falls drops 70 metres through a coffee-plantation gorge surrounded by silver oak trees. The constant mist keeps the surrounding vegetation dripping green year-round.

name: Rann of Kutch
description: The Rann of Kutch is the world's largest salt desert — a vast, dazzling white expanse of 27,900 sq km that floods with shallow seawater during monsoon and dries to a cracked salt flat in winter. The annual Rann Utsav festival celebrates the landscape under the full moon.

hotspots.label: Salt Crystallisation
hotspots.content: The Rann's salt crust forms when seawater infiltrates through cracks in the earth, evaporates, and leaves behind sodium chloride. Local Agaria families have harvested this salt using traditional methods for centuries.

hotspots.label: Greater Flamingo Colony
hotspots.content: The Little Rann of Kutch contains one of the world's largest flamingo breeding colonies. Over 30,000 greater flamingos nest on shallow salt pans. The colony is also home to the only wild Indian wild ass (khur) population.

name: Spiti Valley
description: Spiti Valley is a cold desert mountain valley in the high Himalayas at an average elevation of 3,800 metres. Literally meaning 'Middle Land' (between Tibet and India), it is home to centuries-old Tibetan Buddhist monasteries, fossil-rich terrain, and snow leopards.

hotspots.label: Key Gompa at 4,166m
hotspots.content: Key Monastery is the largest monastery in Spiti Valley, established in the 11th century. It contains ancient thangka paintings, manuscripts, and weaponry. The tiered white structure on the hill is the first landmark visible from the valley.

hotspots.label: Fossil Trilobites & Ammonites
hotspots.content: The Spitian shales contain rich Cambrian and Jurassic marine fossils — trilobites, ammonites, and belemnites — evidence that the Tethys Sea once covered this now bone-dry high desert.

name: Andaman Islands
description: The Andaman Islands are an archipelago of 572 islands in the Bay of Bengal, known for pristine coral reefs, rainforests, and beaches. They are also home to the Cellular Jail — the British colonial prison where Indian freedom fighters were transported — and the world's most protected uncontacted tribes.

hotspots.label: Asia's Best Beach
hotspots.content: Radhanagar Beach on Havelock Island was ranked Asia's best beach by Time magazine. Its turquoise water, white sand, and surrounding forest of giant Andaman Padauk trees create an otherworldly calm.

hotspots.label: Kala Pani Prison
hotspots.content: The seven-winged radial design (1906) allowed 700 solitary cells monitored from a central tower. Over 80,000 Indian freedom fighters were imprisoned here. Veer Savarkar endured 10 years in solitary confinement in Cell 52.

name: Lonar Crater Lake
description: Lonar Crater Lake is the world's only known impact crater in basaltic rock, formed by a meteorite strike approximately 52,000 years ago. The crater's saline-alkaline lake hosts unique extremophile microorganisms and is surrounded by ancient temples at the crater rim.

hotspots.label: 1.8-km Diameter Crater
hotspots.content: The crater is 1.8 km in diameter and 170 metres deep, created by a meteorite strike estimated at 2 million tonnes. The rim offers a complete 360° view of the circular lake below.

hotspots.label: Pink Flamingos & Extremophiles
hotspots.content: The lake's saline-alkaline chemistry (pH ~10) sustains unique cyanobacteria and halophile microorganisms. Flamingos gather seasonally to feed on the algae blooms that turn the water pink during summer.

name: National Museum
description: The National Museum of India in New Delhi houses one of the largest collections of cultural artifacts in Asia — over 200,000 objects spanning 5,000 years of Indian art, archaeological finds, decorative arts, manuscripts, and textiles, including the famous Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-daro.

hotspots.label: Central Dome
hotspots.content: The museum building was designed on classical principles with a central dome inspired by the Sanchi Stupa. The collections span prehistoric stone tools to 20th-century miniature paintings.

hotspots.label: Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-daro
hotspots.content: This 4,500-year-old bronze figurine (10.5 cm) of a young woman with a confident stance, bracelets, and short hair is the most iconic artifact of the Indus Valley Civilisation — the earliest known example of lost-wax casting in the subcontinent.

name: Indian Museum
description: The Indian Museum in Kolkata is the oldest and largest museum in the Indian subcontinent, founded in 1814. Its collection spans natural history, geology, zoology, anthropology, and art — including rare Egyptian mummies, a meteorite weighing 5 tonnes, and the Ashoka Pillar capital.

hotspots.label: 1878 Neoclassical Building
hotspots.content: The museum's present building dates to 1878, designed in Italian Renaissance style with Corinthian columns. The original 1814 establishment makes it the oldest museum in Asia and the Pacific.

hotspots.label: Bharhut Stupa Railings
hotspots.content: The Bharhut Stupa (2nd century BCE) gateway railings show some of the earliest narrative Buddhist sculpture in India. The carvings depict the Jataka tales of the Buddha's previous lives in remarkable detail.

name: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya
description: CSMVS (formerly Prince of Wales Museum) is Mumbai's premier art and natural history museum, housed in a landmark Indo-Saracenic building with a distinctive Mughal dome. Founded in 1922, its 50,000-piece collection includes Indus Valley artifacts, Mughal miniatures, and Chola bronzes.

hotspots.label: Indo-Saracenic Dome
hotspots.content: Architect George Wittet's 1909 design blends Mughal, Maratha, and Jain architectural motifs. The central dome is inspired by the Gol Gumbaz and flanked by chattris. The building itself is a UNESCO-listed heritage structure.

hotspots.label: Nataraja Lost-Wax Bronzes
hotspots.content: The Chola bronzes (9th–13th century) are considered the pinnacle of Indian bronze-casting. The Nataraja (Shiva as cosmic dancer) in its perfect circular frame symbolises the cycle of creation, preservation, and destruction.

name: Salar Jung Museum
description: The Salar Jung Museum is one of the largest museums in India, housing the personal collection of Salar Jung III — a Prime Minister of the Nizam of Hyderabad who spent a lifetime collecting art from across the globe. Its 43,000 objects span 35 galleries and include Aurangzeb's sword, a veiled Rebecca marble sculpture, and a double-faced clock.

hotspots.label: Salar Jung III
hotspots.content: Mir Yousuf Ali Khan Salar Jung III (1889–1949) spent four decades and a personal fortune collecting art during his travels across Europe, the Middle East, and Asia — amassing this collection in 1 man's lifetime.

hotspots.label: Veiled Rebecca Marble
hotspots.content: The most celebrated piece — an Italian marble statue of Rebecca with a translucent veil carved from the same marble block — demonstrates extraordinary skill. The veil appears fabric-thin yet is solid stone.

name: Gateway of India
description: The Gateway of India is a triumphal arch built on the waterfront of Apollo Bunder in 1924 to commemorate the visit of King George V and Queen Mary in 1911. In 1948, the last British troops to leave India marched through this arch — marking the symbolic end of the British Empire in the subcontinent.

hotspots.label: 26-metre Basalt Arch
hotspots.content: Architect George Wittet's 1924 design blends Indo-Saracenic elements with a 26-metre basalt arch. The four turrets were inspired by 16th-century Gujarati architecture. The yellow basalt was imported from Gwalior.

hotspots.label: Elephanta Island Ferry
hotspots.content: Ferries departing from the jetty beside the Gateway reach Elephanta Island in 1 hour — home to 6th-century rock-cut Shiva temples and UNESCO World Heritage status.

name: India Gate
description: India Gate is a 42-metre war memorial designed by Edwin Lutyens to honour the 84,000 Indian soldiers of the British Indian Army who died in World War I and the Third Anglo-Afghan War. The Amar Jawan Jyoti (eternal flame for the unknown soldier) has burned here since 1971, now relocated to the National War Memorial.

hotspots.label: Rajpath to Rashtrapati Bhavan
hotspots.content: The 3-km Kartavya Path (formerly Rajpath) aligns from India Gate directly to Rashtrapati Bhavan on Raisina Hill — Lutyens' grand axial design for Imperial New Delhi. The alignment mirrors the Paris axis from Arc de Triomphe to the Louvre.

hotspots.label: Names of 13,300 Soldiers
hotspots.content: The names of 13,300 British Indian Army soldiers who died on the North-West Frontier (1919) and in Afghanistan are inscribed on the arch's surfaces. Over 70,000 World War I names were added later.

name: Victoria Memorial
description: The Victoria Memorial is a grand white Makrana marble monument built between 1906–1921 to commemorate Queen Victoria's 25-year rule over India. Designed by William Emerson in an Indo-Saracenic style, it now houses a museum of colonial-era artifacts, Raj-period art, and manuscripts.

hotspots.label: Makrana Marble Dome
hotspots.content: The same Makrana marble used for the Taj Mahal clads this 184-metre long structure. The bronze Angel of Victory on the dome rotates with the wind — a curious technical flourish by architect Emerson.

hotspots.label: Thomas Daniell Paintings
hotspots.content: The gallery houses original oil paintings by Thomas and William Daniell who toured India between 1786–1793 producing the definitive visual record of pre-colonial India. Their 'Oriental Scenery' engravings sparked British fascination with Indian landscapes.

name: Howrah Bridge
description: Howrah Bridge (Rabindra Setu) is a cantilever bridge over the Hooghly River, the third longest cantilever bridge in the world when built in 1943. It carries over 100,000 vehicles and innumerable pedestrians daily — making it one of the world's busiest bridges — without a single nut or bolt in its entire steel structure.

hotspots.label: 26,500-tonne Riveted Steel
hotspots.content: The bridge uses 26,500 tonnes of high-tensile steel, entirely held together by 2.3 million rivets. No welding, no bolts. The trusses were fabricated in Calcutta and erected without scaffolding using a cantilever technique.

hotspots.label: Flower Sellers & Devotees
hotspots.content: The Mullik Ghat flower market beneath the Kolkata end of the bridge has operated continuously since colonial times. Tonnes of marigolds, roses, and tuberoses are traded here daily for temples, weddings, and festivals.

name: Nehru Science Centre
description: The Nehru Science Centre in Worli, Mumbai is India's largest interactive science museum. It houses over 500 scientific exhibits across galleries covering space, energy, evolution, and technology. Its 40-metre Bhoomi Vigyan heritage science trail showcases ancient Indian contributions to mathematics and astronomy.

hotspots.label: Outdoor Science Park
hotspots.content: The outdoor science park features a full-scale mock-up of a Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) — the workhorse of India's space programme — and interactive physics demonstration stations.

hotspots.label: Chandrayaan Moon Mission
hotspots.content: The gallery features a detailed exhibit on India's Chandrayaan and Mangalyaan missions — noting that India became the first country to reach Mars orbit on its first attempt and the first to land near the lunar south pole in 2023.

name: Visvesvaraya Industrial & Technological Museum
description: The Visvesvaraya Industrial and Technological Museum in Bengaluru is named after the legendary civil engineer and statesman Sir M. Visvesvaraya. Its eight galleries cover engines, electrotechnology, space, and a fun science gallery, and it is one of India's most visited science museums.

hotspots.label: Sir M. Visvesvaraya Legacy
hotspots.content: Mokshagundam Visvesvaraya (1861–1962) served as Diwan of Mysore and masterminded the Krishna Raja Sagara Dam. He invented an automatic sluice gate system still used globally. His birthday, 15th September, is celebrated as Engineers' Day in India.

hotspots.label: Historic Steam Engines
hotspots.content: The Engine Hall houses a rare working model of James Watt's steam engine alongside actual locomotives from the early Indian Railways era, tracing the evolution of power generation from steam to nuclear.
